denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your provider submitted for temporomandibular joint (TMJ) arthroscopy. After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG), the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 21, 2026.

In applying the Milliman Care Guidelines, our reviewer used the criteria for orthognathic (jaw-realignment) surgery, and the submitted records do not meet the thresholds in that criteria set; on that basis the request does not satisfy our standard for medical necessity. Separately, your plan contains a plan exclusion for the treatment of temporomandibular joint disorders, which independently applies to this request.

A peer-to-peer review window was offered to your provider's office verbally by telephone; that window is not confirmed in this letter. Because the peer-to-peer was not completed, the determination stands at this stage.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
clinical_context: The patient is a 33-year-old woman with refractory internal derangement of the right temporomandibular joint, Wilkes stage III. MRI demonstrates anterior disc displacement without reduction, and she has persistent joint locking with a maximal interincisal mouth opening limited to 24 mm and pain on the right with chewing. Over the past six months she has completed conservative management - a custom stabilization splint worn nightly, twelve sessions of physical therapy with jaw exercises, and a trial of naproxen - without resolution of the mechanical locking. Her oral and maxillofacial surgeon documents that TMJ arthroscopy is indicated for internal derangement with disc displacement refractory to conservative care, and that the orthognathic-surgery criteria the insurer applied govern corrective jaw-realignment osteotomies, not arthroscopic joint procedures, so they were the wrong module for this request. Her coverage is a fully-insured plan issued in her state, where a state mandate requires coverage of temporomandibular joint disorders; that mandate preempts the plan exclusion the insurer cited. Regarding the peer-to-peer review, the surgeon's office received only a verbal telephone notice of the review window and never written confirmation, so the timeframe could not be verified or met.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Cigna, Medical Necessity, surgical, 26-40, F, guideline_mis_cite). TMJ arthroscopy is a surgical procedure; realistic after failed conservative therapy. Rationale is a textbook guideline_mis_cite (orthognathic criteria for an arthroscopic procedure). Age band not 71+, null employer_archetype fine.

applying Milliman Care Guidelines criteria written for orthognathic (jaw-realignment) surgery and concluding the request does not meet those thresholds - the wrong criteria set for an arthroscopic TMJ procedure

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. TMJ arthroscopy for refractory derangement after failed conservative care is standard; Cigna uses MCG/Milliman and applying an orthognathic module to an arthroscopic request is exactly the guideline_mis_cite tactic. TMJ exclusions genuinely collide with state mandates on fully-insured plans; verbal-only P2P is a common procedural defect. Coherent dx/tx/demographics.
evidence_quotes: applying Milliman Care Guidelines criteria written for orthognathic (jaw-realignment) surgery and concluding the request does not meet those thresholds - the wrong criteria set for an arthroscopic TMJ procedure
The letter separately invokes a plan exclusion for the treatment of temporomandibular joint disorders

Logic stays shoddy (unspecified thresholds, conclusory necessity standard) with a realistic layered plan-exclusion. No hallucination.

No invented drugs or impossible timelines.
